questions.question: How do rays parallel to the principal axis behave after reflection from a convex mirror?
questions.option_1: They appear to diverge from a point on the principal axis.
questions.option_2: They get reflected back along the same path.
questions.option_3: They pass through the centre of curvature.
questions.option_4: They actually converge at the focus.
questions.solution: Rays parallel to the principal axis of a convex mirror reflect in such a way that they appear to diverge. After reflection, these rays seem to come from the focal point behind the mirror. Since a convex mirror always forms virtual, erect, and diminished images, the rays do not actually meet but appear to diverge from a virtual focus. Hence, among the options, the correct behaviour is that rays appear to diverge from a point on the principal axis. Final answer: Option 1

questions.question: How does the magnetic field strength at the center of a circular loop change if the current through the loop is doubled?
questions.option_1: It becomes half as large
questions.option_2: It becomes twice as large
questions.option_4: It becomes four times as large
questions.solution: Magnetic field strength (B) at the centre of a circular current loop is directly proportional to the current (I) flowing through it. Formula: B ∝ I If the current is doubled, the magnetic field strength also doubles. Therefore, if current I becomes 2I, magnetic field B becomes 2B. Hence, magnetic field strength becomes twice as large. Final answer: Option 2

questions.question: If a constant force acts on two objects of different masses, which statement best describes their acceleration according to the second law of motion?
questions.option_1: The object with greater mass will have greater acceleration.
questions.option_2: Acceleration does not depend on mass.
questions.option_3: The object with smaller mass will have greater acceleration.
questions.option_4: Both objects will have the same acceleration.
questions.solution: According to Newton's second law of motion, Force = mass × acceleration (F = m × a). For the same constant force, acceleration = Force / mass. Therefore, the object with smaller mass will have greater acceleration because acceleration is inversely proportional to mass when force is constant. Hence, option 3 is correct. Final answer: Option 3

questions.question: If a convex mirror forms a point-sized image at its focus, where is the object located?
questions.option_1: At the pole
questions.option_2: Between P and F
questions.option_4: Between F and the pole
questions.solution: A convex mirror always forms a virtual, erect, and diminished image of an object. The image formed by a convex mirror is always located behind the mirror, and at the focus only when the object is at infinity. When the object is at infinity, the reflected rays appear to come from the focus, forming a point-sized image there. Therefore, the object must be located at infinity to form a point-sized image at the focus of a convex mirror. Final answer: Option 3

questions.question: If a material has an absolute refractive index of 1.7, what does this imply about the speed of light in that material compared to vacuum?
questions.option_1: Light travels at the same speed in both.
questions.option_2: Light travels 1.7 times faster in the material than in vacuum.
questions.option_3: The speed difference cannot be determined from this value.
questions.option_4: Light travels 1.7 times slower in the material than in vacuum.
questions.solution: The absolute refractive index (n) is the ratio of speed of light in vacuum (c) to speed of light in the material (v). n = c / v Given n = 1.7, so light travels in the material slower than in vacuum. Speed in material = c / 1.7, which means light is 1.7 times slower in the material than in vacuum. Therefore, option 4 is correct. Final answer: Option 4

questions.question: If a point is located below the principal axis of a spherical mirror, what sign is assigned to its perpendicular distance according to the sign convention for spherical mirrors?
questions.option_1: Depends on mirror type
questions.option_2: Negative
questions.option_4: Zero
questions.solution: According to the sign convention for spherical mirrors, distances measured below the principal axis are assigned a negative sign. This applies for perpendicular distances from the principal axis. Therefore, if a point lies below the principal axis, its perpendicular distance is negative. Final answer: Option 2

questions.question: If an ammeter reading increases when a thicker wire replaces a thinner one of the same material and length, what is the most likely reason?
questions.option_1: The voltage across the wire decreased
questions.option_2: The resistance of the thicker wire is lower, allowing more current
questions.option_4: The wire's resistivity increased
questions.solution: Thicker wire has larger cross-sectional area. Resistance R = ρL/A, where A increases means R decreases. Lower resistance allows more current through the wire. Ammeter reads current; increased current means lower resistance. Hence, thicker wire causes lower resistance, resulting in increased current reading. Final answer: Option 2

questions.question: If atmospheric refraction did not occur, which change would be most noticeable during the observation of stars from Earth?
questions.option_1: Stars would change color rapidly.
questions.option_2: Stars would disappear from the night sky.
questions.option_4: Stars would not twinkle; their light would remain steady.
questions.solution: Atmospheric refraction causes the bending of starlight as it passes through Earth's atmosphere. This bending leads to the apparent twinkling (scintillation) of stars. Without atmospheric refraction, starlight would not bend or be distorted. Therefore, stars would not appear to twinkle but shine with a steady light. Planets do not twinkle much because they have a larger apparent size, averaging out atmospheric effects. Hence, the most noticeable change would be the disappearance of the twinkling effect in stars. Correct answer is option 4. Answer complete.

questions.question: If the current in a wire is 2 amperes, how much charge flows through a cross-section in 5 seconds?
questions.option_1: 7 coulombs
questions.option_2: 10 coulombs
questions.option_4: 0.4 coulombs
questions.solution: Current (I) is the rate of flow of charge (Q) through a conductor. The relation is Q = I × t where, I = 2 amperes (current) t = 5 seconds (time) Calculate charge: Q = 2 × 5 Q = 10 coulombs So, 10 coulombs of charge flows in 5 seconds. Final answer: Option 2

questions.question: If two cars start at the same point and move along a straight road, Car X at a constant speed of 40 km/h and Car Y at a constant speed of 60 km/h, which statement correctly compares their motions after 2 hours?
questions.option_1: Car Y will be 20 km ahead of Car X after 2 hours.
questions.option_2: Both cars will have traveled the same distance after 2 hours.
questions.option_3: Car X will be 40 km ahead of Car Y after 2 hours.
questions.option_4: Car Y will be 40 km ahead of Car X after 2 hours.
questions.solution: Solution: Distance = Speed × Time Car X: 40 × 2 = 80 km Car Y: 60 × 2 = 120 km Difference = 120 − 80 = 40 km So Car Y is 40 km ahead of Car X . Answer: Car Y will be 40 km ahead of Car X after 2 hours.

questions.question: In a parallel combination of three resistors, which statement correctly describes the total current in the circuit?
questions.option_1: It is the average of the currents through each resistor
questions.option_2: It is equal to the sum of the currents through each resistor
questions.option_3: It is the same as the current through each resistor
questions.option_4: It is equal to the current through the resistor with the lowest resistance
questions.solution: In a parallel circuit, the total current is the sum of the currents through each resistor. Each resistor provides a separate path for the current. Therefore, the total current equals the sum of individual branch currents. This follows the principle of parallel circuits in electricity. Hence, the correct statement is that total current is the sum of currents through each resistor. Final answer: Option 2

questions.question: In the context of uniform circular motion, which of the following examples from everyday life best illustrates this concept?
questions.option_1: A car moving with changing speed along a circular track
questions.option_2: A person walking randomly in a park
questions.option_3: A satellite revolving around the Earth at a fixed speed in a circular orbit
questions.option_4: A pendulum bob swinging to and fro
questions.solution: The concept of uniform circular motion refers to an object moving in a circle at a constant speed. Option 1 is incorrect because the car's speed is changing, so the motion is not uniform. Option 2 describes random walking, not circular motion. Option 3 correctly describes a satellite moving at a constant speed along a circular orbit around the Earth, which is uniform circular motion. Option 4 describes a pendulum, which moves in a back-and-forth arc, not a full circular path at constant speed. Hence, option 3 best illustrates uniform circular motion. Final answer: Option 3

questions.question: Ozone shields the surface of the Earth from which type of radiation coming from the Sun?
questions.option_1: White light
questions.option_2: X rays
questions.option_4: Ultra violet
questions.solution: Ozone layer is located in the Earth's stratosphere. It absorbs the majority of the Sun's harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation. This protection prevents most UV rays from reaching the Earth's surface. UV radiation can cause skin cancer and other health issues. Hence, the ozone layer acts as a shield against ultraviolet radiation from the Sun. Correct answer is Option 4: Ultra violet.

questions.question: Suppose a student wants to design an amusement park ride that moves people in a circle at uniform speed. Which concept should the student apply to ensure riders stay safely in their seats during the circular motion?
questions.option_1: Increasing speed randomly will keep them safe
questions.option_2: A constant outward force is needed to keep riders pressed against their seats
questions.option_3: A constant inward (centripetal) force is needed on the riders to keep them in uniform circular motion
questions.option_4: Gravity alone is sufficient to keep the riders in place
questions.solution: Correct option is 3 To keep riders safely in their seats during circular motion, a constant inward force (centripetal force) is required. This inward force acts towards the centre of the circle and maintains uniform circular motion. It prevents riders from flying outward due to inertia. Therefore, the ride must provide centripetal force to keep riders moving in a circle safely. Options 1, 2 and 4 are incorrect because: 1 is unsafe as randomly increasing speed does not provide controlled force. 2 is wrong because the force is inward, not outward. 4 is incorrect as gravity alone cannot provide the necessary centripetal force. This is a fundamental principle in circular motion in physics. Answer is Option 3.

questions.question: What does Snell's law of refraction state about the ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction for a given colour and pair of media?
questions.option_1: It depends on the type of mirror.
questions.option_2: It varies with the normal.
questions.option_3: It changes with the angle of incidence.
questions.option_4: It remains constant.
questions.solution: Snell's law states that for a given colour and pair of media, the ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is constant. This constant is called the refractive index of the second medium with respect to the first medium. Therefore, the ratio does not depend on the angle of incidence or normal but remains constant for a specific colour and media pair. Hence, the correct option is that it remains constant. Final answer: Option 4

questions.question: What happens to the potential energy of an object if its mass is halved while its height above the ground remains constant?
questions.option_1: The potential energy becomes zero
questions.option_2: The potential energy is halved
questions.option_4: The potential energy is doubled
questions.solution: Potential energy (PE) is given by the formula: PE = mgh, where: m = mass of the object, g = acceleration due to gravity, h = height above the ground. If the mass is halved, m becomes m/2, while g and h remain constant. So, new potential energy = (m/2) × g × h = (1/2) × original potential energy. Therefore, the potential energy is halved when the mass is halved, keeping height constant. Final answer: Option 2

questions.question: What happens to the work done if the displacement becomes zero despite a force being applied?
questions.option_1: The work done remains constant.
questions.option_2: The work done decreases.
questions.option_4: The work done is zero.
questions.solution: Work done is calculated by the formula: Work = Force × Displacement × cos(θ). If displacement is zero, then Work = Force × 0 × cos(θ) = 0. Thus, no matter how much force is applied, if there is no displacement, the work done is zero. Therefore, the correct answer is option 4.

questions.question: What is the reason why electrons move in a conducting wire when connected to a battery, but not when the wire is left untouched?
questions.option_1: A battery creates a potential difference that sets electrons in motion
questions.option_2: Gravity acts on electrons in the wire
questions.option_4: Electrons have intrinsic energy for movement
questions.solution: When a conducting wire is connected to a battery, the battery creates a potential difference (voltage) across the wire. This potential difference causes electrons to move, generating an electric current. If the wire is left untouched (not connected to a battery), there is no potential difference to push the electrons, so they do not move. Gravity does not have a significant effect on electrons in the wire, and simply being made of copper does not cause electron movement. Electrons do not move spontaneously without an external force like a battery. Final answer: Option 1

questions.question: What is the sign of the focal length for a convex lens as per the sign convention for spherical lenses?
questions.option_1: Positive
questions.option_2: Negative
questions.option_4: Variable
questions.solution: For spherical lenses, the sign convention states: The focal length (f) is positive for a convex lens. This is because a convex lens converges light rays, hence it has a positive focal length. Concave lenses have negative focal lengths as per the sign convention. Therefore, the focal length of a convex lens is positive. Final answer: Option 1

questions.question: What role does the earth wire play in the safety of domestic appliances with metallic bodies?
questions.option_1: It prevents the fuse from melting
questions.option_2: It controls the current flow
questions.option_4: It provides a low-resistance path for leakage current
questions.solution: The earth wire in domestic appliances is connected to the metallic body. It provides a low-resistance path to the ground for any leakage current. This prevents electric shock to the user if the live wire touches the metal body. Hence, it enhances safety by directing leakage current safely to the earth. So, the earth wire's main role is to provide a low-resistance path for leakage current. Final answer: Option 4

questions.question: When a gun is fired, it recoils backward as the bullet moves forward. How does this demonstrate Newton’s third law of motion?
questions.option_1: The gun recoils because the bullet moves faster than the gun.
questions.option_2: The bullet moves forward only because the gun has stored energy.
questions.option_3: The gun pushes the bullet forward, and the bullet pushes the gun backward with an equal and opposite force.
questions.option_4: The gun moves backward because it is heavier than the bullet.
questions.solution: When a gun is fired, it exerts a force on the bullet, pushing it forward. According to Newton’s third law, for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. Thus, as the gun pushes the bullet forward, the bullet pushes the gun backward with equal force. This backward movement of the gun is called recoil and directly demonstrates Newton’s third law of motion. Hence, the forces between the gun and the bullet are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction. Final answer: Option 3

questions.question: When a light ray enters a rectangular glass slab obliquely from air, what happens to its direction at the first interface?
questions.option_1: It gets reflected back
questions.option_2: It bends away from the normal
questions.option_4: It bends towards the normal
questions.solution: When light passes from a less dense medium (air) to a denser medium (glass), it slows down. Due to this change in speed, it bends towards the normal at the interface. Hence, the ray bends towards the normal when entering the glass slab obliquely from air. Option 4 is correct. Final answer: Option 4

questions.question: When a man pushes a wall but the wall does not move, what is the work done by the man on the wall?
questions.option_1: Maximum work is done as the man uses full effort.
questions.option_2: Zero work is done as there is no displacement of the wall.
questions.option_3: Positive work is done as force is applied.
questions.option_4: Negative work is done as the wall resists the push.
questions.solution: Work done is defined as force applied multiplied by displacement in the direction of the force. Here, the man applies force on the wall but the wall does not move. Since displacement is zero, no work is done on the wall. Therefore, work done by the man on the wall is zero. Final answer: Option 2

questions.question: When an object is in free fall near the Earth's surface, which of the following physical quantities remains constant if air resistance is neglected?
questions.option_1: Momentum remains constant.
questions.option_2: Velocity remains constant.
questions.option_4: Acceleration due to gravity remains constant.
questions.solution: When an object is in free fall near the Earth's surface, the only quantity that remains constant (ignoring air resistance) is acceleration due to gravity. The acceleration due to gravity (g) is approximately 9.8 m/s² and acts downwards throughout the fall. Momentum and velocity change because the object speeds up as it falls. Energy changes form from potential to kinetic during the fall. Hence, acceleration due to gravity is the only constant. Final answer: Option 4

questions.question: When an object is placed at infinity in front of a convex mirror, what is the nature of the image formed?
questions.option_1: Real and inverted
questions.option_2: Real and erect
questions.option_4: Virtual and erect
questions.solution: The image formed by a convex mirror is always virtual and erect. When the object is placed at infinity, the image forms at the focal point of the mirror. This image is diminished in size but remains virtual and upright (erect). Hence, the correct nature of the image is virtual and erect. Final answer: Option 4
